headline: NATURAL GAS SPIKES AS GRAINS HOLD STEADY
subheadline: Energy volatility returns while crop futures mark time during planting season.
lead: Grain futures drifted in narrow ranges Thursday as corn reached $4.64 per bushel and December corn climbed to $4.84. Natural gas provided the day's fireworks with a 5.5% surge to $2.87, breaking a string of quiet energy sessions. Planting pressure kept crop futures anchored despite decent demand underneath.
teaser: Gas spikes 5.5% while corn and beans treade water in planting season doldrums.

one_number.context: Gas jumped 5.5% in its biggest single-day move since March, driven by revised cooling degree day forecasts and storage concerns. The spike reminds producers that energy costs can turn volatile fast. Diesel and propane often follow gas moves with a lag during peak planting and application season.

sections.title: Grains Mark Time
sections.body: Corn gained modestly to $4.64 nearby while December futures added four cents to $4.84, reflecting ongoing planting pressure and solid demand. Soybeans eased a penny to $11.80 as November contracts held flat at $11.58. Chicago wheat firmed to $6.13 as spring planting delays overseas support the complex. Calendar spreads remain tight, suggesting nearby supplies stay firm despite adequate old crop stocks.
sections.bottom_line: Grains consolidating in tight ranges as planting season dominates price discovery.

sections.title: Livestock Pressure
sections.body: Live cattle slipped 0.1% to $243.22 as packers maintain pressure ahead of weekend kills. Feeder cattle held steady at $359.00 despite stronger grain costs eating into margins. Lean hogs dropped half a percent to $102.50 on seasonal weakness patterns. Class III milk bucked the trend, gaining 0.3% to $17.51 as spring flush peaks support nearby prices. Protein complexes remain under pressure from adequate supplies.
sections.bottom_line: Livestock markets steady to weaker as seasonal patterns dominate trading.

sections.title: Energy Volatility Returns
sections.body: Natural gas exploded 5.5% to $2.87 in its biggest single-day move since March, catching traders off guard after weeks of sideways action. Revised cooling forecasts and storage injection concerns drove the spike. WTI crude oil eased 0.8% to $93.84 as refinery maintenance season continues. Energy volatility often clusters, and today's gas move could signal more erratic pricing ahead during peak application season.
sections.bottom_line: Gas spike breaks energy calm with implications for input costs.
sections.farmer_action: Lock remaining propane and diesel if natural gas holds above $2.75 through Friday close.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Natural Gas Moves Matter for Agriculture
the_more_you_know.body: Natural gas prices directly impact nitrogen fertilizer costs since gas accounts for 70% to 80% of ammonia production expenses. A 10% gas price increase typically translates to a 6% to 8% rise in anhydrous ammonia within 30 to 60 days. Gas also affects propane costs for grain drying and heating livestock facilities. Today's 5.5% spike signals potential input cost pressure heading into peak application season. Smart producers watch gas futures as a leading indicator for fall fertilizer pricing.

daily_quote.text: The most dangerous phrase in the language is: we've always done it this way.
